content.full_text:
FILE

RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

July 18, 1995

Mr. David Blakely
Post Office Box 909
Santa Margarita, California 93453

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-95-202

Dear Mr. Blakely:

We respond to your request for formal written advice
regarding a possible conflict of interest under the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Do you have a conflict of interest in decisions of the Board
of Supervisors of San Luis Obispo County regarding the Santa
Margarita Ranch based upon the fact that your personal residence
is located within 300 feet of the boundaries of the Santa
Margarita Ranch?

ANSWER

Yes. Based on the facts you have provided, you have a
conflict of interest in any decision regarding development of the
Santa Margarita Ranch.

FACTS

You are a County Supervisor in San Luis Obispo County. You
own real property within a 300 foot radius of the Santa Margarita
Ranch (the "Ranch"). The Ranch is approximately 14,000 acres in
size. The county is considering elements of the General Plan as
it relates to the Ranch. The elements of the General Plan will
affect the rules and procedures for the Ranch owners to apply for
a future general plan and specific plan amendment. Although no
specific plan for development is currently before the Board of
Supervisors, the proposed plan attempts to limit the types of uses

________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18995.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-202
Page 2


that the Ranch owners may apply for in the future. Accordingly,
the decision currently before the Board of Supervisors will affect
the nature, scope, location and density of future development
allowed on the Ranch.

ANALYSIS

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit a
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
attempting to influence a governmental decision in which the
official knows or has reason to know that he has a financial
interest. (Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest
in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, when it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the decision's effects on
the public generally, on any one of five enumerated financial
interests, including real property owned by the official worth
$1000 or more. (Section 87103.) A conflict of interest exists
when the necessary elements, financial interest, reasonable
foreseeability, materiality, and an effect distinguishable from
the effects on the public generally, exist. We address each
element separately.

A. Financial Interest

You have stated that your personal residence lies within 300
feet of the Ranch and that the value of your residence is greater
than $1000.2 Under the statutory definition, your residence is a
financial interest which may be grounds for disqualification if
the remaining elements are present. (Section 87103.)

B. Reasonable Foreseeability

The Commission has defined reasonable foreseeability in a
formal opinion, In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC 198. In that opinion,
the Commission held that whether or not a decision's effects are
reasonably foreseeable is a question of fact. While absolute
certainty is not required, a mere possibility is insufficient to
raise to the level of being reasonably foreseeable. Therefore, the
Commission determined that an effect is reasonably foreseeable
when it is substantially likely to occur. We use that definition
in determining whether a decision regarding development of a

2
You have also indicated that the owners of the Ranch have
proffered an ancient map on which indicates that your residence is
actually further than 300 feet from the Ranch. However, the
validity of this ancient map is disputed by the county, and no
judicial determination of its validity has been sought. In
rendering our advice, we have taken the facts as they exist at
this time. We do not speculate on the validity of the ancient map
or its effects if found to be valid at some later date. It is
undisputed at this time that your residence is actually within 300
feet of the boundaries of the Ranch.

File No. A-95-202
Page 3


14,000 acre ranch located within 300 feet of your residence will
result in any reasonably foreseeable financial effects on your
property interest.

Your personal residence is within 300 feet of the Ranch. The
Ranch is currently zoned as agricultural. The owners of the Ranch
wish to maximize the allowable development of the Ranch. If
logically, increased development affects surrounding property by
changing the character and traits of the community. Conversely,
limited development may preserve aspects of the community that
benefit surrounding home owners. Therefore, it is reasonable
foreseeable that any change in the General Plan which would affect
the allowable development of the Ranch would have some reasonably
foreseeable economic effect on your property. (See Rudansky
Advice Letter, No. I-90-429 [decision which may affect development
within 300 feet of an official's residence is substantially likely
to have an economic effect on the official's property].)
Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision pending
before the Board of Supervisors regarding the General Plan, as it
relates to the Ranch, will affect your financial interest.

C. Materiality

As some economic effect on your residence is reasonably
foreseeable, we must examine whether that effect is likely to be
material. The Commission has adopted regulations defining when an
effect is material. Regulation 18702.3 provides that when an
official's property is indirectly affected by a decision, and is
located within 300 feet from property that is the subject of the
decision, the decision's reasonably foreseeable effects are deemed
material unless the decision will have absolutely no effect on the
official's property.

It is logical to assume that a change in the use of the Ranch
will result in a change to the character of the surrounding area,
including effects on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use,
noise levels, air emissions, and similar neighborhood traits. You
have provided no facts which would indicate that these foreseeable
effects will not occur, or that no financial effect on your
property will result therefrom. Therefore, application of
Regulation 18702.3 to your facts results in a determination that
the effect of the decision currently before the Board of
Supervisors regarding the Ranch will have a material financial
effect on your residence.

D. The Public Generally Exception

An official with a financial interest that is substantially
likely to be materially affected by a governmental decision may
still participate in that decision if the reasonably foreseeable
effects are indistinguishable from the effects the decision will
have on the public generally. (Section 87103.) Regulation 18703
defines when an effect on an official's financial interest is
indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Under

File No. A-95-202
Page 4


Regulation 18703, an effect on a public official's financial
interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public
generally when the decision will affect 10 percent or more of the
population, property owners, home owners or households, or 5,000
individual residents of the district the official represents, in
substantially the same manner as it affects the official's
interest.

You were elected to represent District 5, which has a
population of approximately 44,000.³ Therefore, in order for the
public generally exception to apply in the instant case, there
would have to be at least 4,400 people, property owners, home
owners, or households in the district you represent with real
property located within 300 feet of the Ranch. In our telephone
conversations, you indicated that this was not the case.
Accordingly, the public generally exception does not allow your
participation in the general plan amendment as it relates to the
Ranch.

E. Using Official Position To Influence A Decision

An official disqualified from making and participating in the
making of a governmental decision is also disqualified from using
his or her official position to influence the outcome of a
decision. (Section 87100.) An official is attempting to
influence the outcome of a decision when, for the purpose of
influencing a decision, the official contacts, appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or
consultant of the official's agency or any agency appointed or
subject of budgetary control of the official's agency.
(Regulation 18700.1(a).)

However, an official who is otherwise disqualified from a
decision may appear before his agency as any other member of the
general public solely to represent himself or herself on a matter
related to his or her personal interests, including matters
affecting real property owned by the official. (Regulation
18700.1(b).) Accordingly, you may appear before the Board of
Supervisors in a public meeting as a concerned or interested
member of the public to express your views on development of the
Ranch.


________________________
3    This approximation is based on Ms. Ann McMahon's
representation that the county population is 220,000 and the
county is divided into five equally populated districts.

File No. A-95-202
Page 5


We trust that this advice letter provides you with the
assistance you seek. Should you have any further questions you
may contact the undersigned at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
[signature]
By: Daniel E. Muallem
Counsel, Legal Division
